title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Remote Work on Corporate Culture

texts.A: The shift to remote work has permanently severed the vital ties that bind a workforce together. The casual, unscheduled interactions that once occurred in corridors and communal kitchens were far more valuable than any management framework ever acknowledged—they were the connective tissue of organizational life. [37]Spontaneous collaboration, the lifeblood of innovation, cannot be replicated on a video call.[/37] When employees are isolated, their connection to the company's core mission withers, and the subtle cultural transmission that sustains organizational identity gradually disappears. [38]While some tout the benefits of flexibility, the long-term cost is a fragmented culture and a loss of institutional knowledge.[/38] [40]Mentorship, in particular, has suffered; junior staff no longer benefit from the 'osmosis' of watching senior leaders navigate complex challenges in real-time.[/40] This intangible apprenticeship model developed generations of competent professionals, and no formal training programme can adequately compensate for its absence. The erosion of this culture is not hypothetical; it is already visible in declining cross-departmental collaboration and rising rates of disconnection reported in employee engagement surveys across every major industry.
texts.B: The traditional office was never the crucible of culture we imagine it to be; for many, it was a site of performative productivity, personality-driven politics, and unnecessary stress that consumed energy without generating proportionate value. [40]Remote work has democratized the professional sphere, allowing merit to take precedence over physical presence.[/40] The 'loss of innovation' argument is a fallacy pushed by those who fear a loss of control over a workforce that has proven it does not need constant supervision to perform effectively. [37]With the right digital tools, global teams can collaborate more effectively than those limited by a shared physical zip code.[/37] The future is asynchronous and output-focused. Organizations that resist this reality will find themselves unable to compete for talent in a market where flexibility has become a fundamental expectation rather than a discretionary perk. The companies that will thrive in the next decade are those that build cultures around shared purpose and measurable impact rather than shared geography and the performative rituals of office attendance.
texts.C: We are currently in a messy transition phase, but the 'hybrid' model is the only sustainable path forward. Organizations that are thoughtful about this balance will attract and retain talent that both mono-cultural extremes will ultimately drive away. [39]Total remote work leads to the isolation described by critics, but a total return to the office is an affront to the work-life balance employees now rightfully demand.[/39] The key is intentionality. Offices should be redesigned as 'hubs' for specific collaborative events—workshops, creative sprints, and team-building sessions—while deep, focused work happens at home. Those who insist on one extreme or the other are ignoring the nuanced reality of modern labor psychology. The research is unambiguous: autonomy is the single greatest predictor of employee engagement, and any policy that fails to account for individual differences in working style will ultimately underperform. Organizations willing to redesign both their spaces and their cultures will discover that the hybrid model is not a compromise but a genuinely superior way of organizing collaborative, human-centred work.
texts.D: Arguments against remote work often mask a deep-seated lack of trust in employees. However, setting aside questions of managerial control, we must acknowledge that humans are fundamentally social animals whose psychological wellbeing depends heavily on regular, informal, face-to-face interaction. [39]The lack of physical proximity leads to a subtle but measurable decline in 'social capital'—the trust and empathy built during coffee breaks and hallway chats.[/39] Without this capital, conflict resolution becomes harder, misunderstandings proliferate, and the goodwill that enables teams to weather crises gradually erodes. [38]While productivity might remain high in the short term, the social fabric of the organization eventually thins, making it harder to retain talent during turbulent times.[/38] The hidden cost of remote work is not measured in quarterly output figures but in the slow degradation of organizational cohesion that only becomes visible when it is already too late to repair. Investing in regular, structured in-person time is not a concession to nostalgia but a strategically sound response to the measurable limitations of digital communication for complex interpersonal and organizational dynamics.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B regarding the impact of physical presence on collaboration?
questions.explanation: B argues that digital tools allow global teams to work better than those in the same zip code. A argues that spontaneous collaboration 'cannot be replicated' without being physically together.

questions.question: Which expert shares D's concern about the social consequences of long-term remote work?
questions.explanation: D mentions a decline in 'social capital' and 'social fabric.' A similarly notes that the 'vital ties' are severed and the workforce becomes 'fragmented.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar opinion to C about the flaws of a 'pure' remote work model?
questions.explanation: C states that 'total remote work leads to isolation.' D agrees that 'lack of physical proximity' causes a decline in trust and social connection.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the effect of remote work on junior employees' development?
questions.explanation: A claims mentorship has suffered because junior staff can't learn by osmosis. B argues that remote work 'democratizes' the sphere and allows 'merit to take precedence,' implying a more fair environment for all to succeed.
